sections.plain_summary: Nickel sensitivity — also called Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome (SNAS) — is a condition in which the immune system reacts to nickel both on the skin and when nickel is ingested through food or water. Most people associate nickel allergy with contact dermatitis from jewellery or belt buckles, but those with SNAS experience broader, systemic symptoms triggered by nickel in the diet. Because nickel is present in many everyday foods — including wholegrains, legumes, nuts, seeds, and chocolate — digestive symptoms, skin flares, and fatigue can seem completely unrelated to metal exposure, making the condition difficult to recognise.
sections.detailed_summary:
Nickel is the most common cause of contact allergic dermatitis worldwide, affecting an estimated 8–15% of the population to varying degrees. In a subset of those individuals, nickel sensitivity extends beyond skin contact to produce systemic reactions driven by dietary nickel intake. This broader presentation is classified as Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome (SNAS).

The mechanism is thought to involve a T-cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity response — the same type IV immune pathway responsible for contact dermatitis — but triggered internally when nickel absorbed from food reaches sensitised tissues. Because nickel is an essential trace element found in soil, water, and a wide range of plant foods, complete avoidance is impractical, but reducing dietary intake below an individual threshold can dramatically reduce symptom burden in those who are reactive.

Gastrointestinal symptoms are central to SNAS and frequently lead affected individuals to be investigated for IBS, SIBO, or food intolerances before nickel is considered. Typical gut symptoms include nausea, bloating, abdominal pain, and altered bowel habits. Skin manifestations — including dyshidrotic eczema on the palms and soles, widespread eczematous rashes, and urticaria — are also common, and may worsen or improve in parallel with dietary changes.

Diagnosis typically begins with a positive patch test to nickel sulfate, combined with a structured low-nickel dietary trial. Oral nickel challenge tests can be used in specialist settings to confirm dietary reactivity. No single biomarker reliably identifies SNAS; clinical correlation between symptoms, patch test results, and dietary response is essential.

Management centres on reducing dietary nickel load — a more nuanced process than simply eliminating one food group, as nickel content varies with food type, cooking method, and even the cookware used. Working with a dietitian experienced in metal sensitivity or a specialist allergist or dermatologist is important for structuring this safely and without unnecessary nutritional restriction.

prevalence.summary: Nickel contact allergy is common; SNAS — the systemic dietary form — affects a meaningful subset of those sensitised.
prevalence.estimate: Contact nickel allergy: 8–15% of the general population. SNAS (systemic dietary reactivity): estimated 1–2% of the population, representing perhaps 10–30% of those with confirmed nickel contact allergy.
prevalence.notes: Prevalence data for SNAS specifically are limited and largely derived from European dermatology and allergy clinics. Women are diagnosed more frequently than men, likely reflecting greater jewellery exposure, though hormonal factors may also play a role. Rates have increased over recent decades, attributed to rising nickel exposure through body piercing, jewellery, dietary changes, and occupational contact. Many cases of SNAS are likely undiagnosed, misclassified as IBS, or attributed to unrelated food intolerances.

subgroups.name: Contact-only nickel allergy
subgroups.description: Individuals who react to nickel through skin contact — from jewellery, clothing fasteners, phones, or tools — but do not experience significant symptoms from dietary nickel intake.
distinguishing_features: Rash confined to areas of metal contact (earlobes, wrist, abdomen under belt buckle)
Symptoms resolve fully with avoidance of contact sources
Patch test positive; dietary challenge does not reproduce symptoms

subgroups.name: Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome (SNAS) — GI-predominant
subgroups.description: Individuals whose primary symptoms from dietary nickel are gastrointestinal: nausea, bloating, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, or constipation. This group is frequently misdiagnosed with IBS.
distinguishing_features: Positive patch test to nickel sulfate
Symptoms triggered or worsened by high-nickel meals
GI symptoms may occur with or without skin flares
Partial or full response to low-nickel diet

subgroups.name: SNAS — skin-predominant
subgroups.description: Individuals whose systemic nickel reactivity manifests primarily as skin conditions — particularly dyshidrotic eczema (pompholyx) on hands and feet — that flare with dietary nickel.
distinguishing_features: Vesicular, intensely itchy blisters on palms, fingers, and soles
Flares correspond to high-nickel dietary periods
Patch test strongly positive; skin improves significantly on low-nickel diet

subgroups.name: SNAS — multi-system
subgroups.description: Individuals who experience both GI and skin symptoms alongside systemic features such as fatigue, brain fog, and headaches when dietary nickel load is high.
distinguishing_features: Broad symptom profile spanning gut, skin, and neurological/fatigue domains
Often the most complex diagnostic presentation
May overlap with MCAS or other multi-system sensitivities
Requires careful dietary trial to disentangle nickel from other triggers

symptoms.notes: Often occurs within hours of a high-nickel meal; can mimic IBS symptoms closely
May accompany or follow meals heavy in wholegrains, legumes, or nuts
Colicky, diffuse, or localised; can be mistaken for functional gut disorders
Variable; some individuals experience constipation or alternating bowel habits
Small, intensely itchy vesicles on palms, fingers, and soles; strongly associated with dietary nickel load
May appear on trunk, flexures, or limbs; flares often correspond to dietary triggers rather than contact
Raised, itchy welts; can be acute or chronic and recurrent
Rash at earlobes, wrists, navel, finger where ring is worn — often the first recognised symptom of nickel allergy
Persistent tiredness that may worsen in the days following high-nickel dietary exposure
Reported in multi-system presentations; mechanism not fully established
More common in severe SNAS; may correlate with symptom flares
Occasionally reported as part of systemic reactivity; may overlap with other allergic conditions
Reported in some multi-system cases; not well-characterised in the literature

key_patterns: Positive patch test to nickel sulfate (typically 5% in petrolatum) confirms nickel contact sensitisation
Symptom improvement on a structured low-nickel diet trial (typically 4–6 weeks) suggests dietary reactivity
Symptom return on reintroduction of high-nickel foods helps confirm dietary trigger
GI symptoms that parallel skin flares — especially hand eczema — raise suspicion for SNAS rather than purely functional gut disorders
History of metal jewellery reactions predating GI or systemic symptoms is a key clue
Symptoms unresponsive to typical IBS interventions (low-FODMAP, antispasmodics) but partially improved by dietary change warrant nickel investigation

at_home.name: Dietary nickel journal
at_home.description: Keeping a detailed food and symptom diary, noting intake of high-nickel foods (oats, wholegrains, legumes, nuts, seeds, dark chocolate, soy products, spinach) alongside skin and GI symptoms. Patterns linking high-nickel days to symptom flares within 12–48 hours can be informative.
at_home.safety_notes: This is an observational tool only. Do not undertake a strict elimination diet without guidance — unnecessarily restricting wholegrains and legumes can impair nutritional adequacy.

at_home.name: Dimethylglyoxime (DMG) nickel spot test
at_home.description: A simple chemical test available as inexpensive test kits that can detect nickel in jewellery, cookware, piercings, and everyday objects. A pink-red colour indicates nickel release. Useful for identifying contact sources in the environment.
at_home.safety_notes: This tests objects, not body reactions. A positive test on an object does not confirm SNAS — it only confirms nickel presence in that item.

clinical.name: Patch test — nickel sulfate
clinical.description: The standard diagnostic test for nickel contact allergy. A small amount of nickel sulfate solution is applied under an occlusive patch to the upper back for 48 hours, then read at 48 and 96 hours. A positive result (erythema, induration, vesicles at the patch site) confirms type IV delayed hypersensitivity to nickel. This is necessary but not sufficient to diagnose SNAS — dietary reactivity must be demonstrated separately.
clinical.availability: Performed by dermatologists and allergists. Some allergy clinics offer extended patch test panels. Not routinely available in all primary care settings.

clinical.name: Supervised low-nickel dietary trial
clinical.description: A structured reduction in dietary nickel intake over 4–6 weeks, ideally guided by a dietitian familiar with metal sensitivity. Foods highest in nickel (oats, wholegrain flour, legumes, nuts, seeds, cocoa/chocolate, soy, shellfish, and certain vegetables) are reduced, and symptom response is tracked. This is the primary way to establish whether systemic symptoms are nickel-driven.
clinical.availability: Requires guidance from a dietitian or allergist. Published low-nickel food tables (such as those from Italian SNAS research groups) can help structure the trial.

clinical.name: Oral nickel challenge test
clinical.description: An oral dose of nickel sulfate (typically 0.5–5 mg of elemental nickel) is administered under clinical supervision, and skin and systemic symptoms are monitored over 24–72 hours. A positive result reproduces the patient's typical symptoms. Used to confirm dietary reactivity and is considered the most rigorous method for diagnosing SNAS beyond patch testing alone.
clinical.availability: Available in specialist allergy and dermatology centres, primarily in Europe (particularly Italy, where SNAS research has been most active). Not widely available in all countries. Requires specialist referral.

clinical.name: Serum or urine nickel levels
clinical.description: Measurement of nickel concentration in blood or urine. Elevated levels may reflect high dietary or occupational exposure but do not confirm clinical sensitivity. Research use is more established than diagnostic use. Not routinely used in standard clinical workup for SNAS.
clinical.availability: Available through some occupational health and environmental medicine labs. Interpretation requires specialist input.

clinical.name: Lymphocyte transformation test (LTT)
clinical.description: An in-vitro test measuring T-lymphocyte proliferation in response to nickel exposure. Can detect sensitisation at a cellular level. Used in some European centres as a supplementary test, particularly when patch testing is ambiguous or cannot be performed.
clinical.availability: Limited availability; primarily research or specialist allergy centres in Europe. Not standard practice in most clinical settings.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: SNAS-related gut symptoms — bloating, abdominal pain, altered stool pattern — closely resemble IBS. Many people with SNAS are diagnosed with IBS before dietary nickel is considered. Unlike IBS, SNAS symptoms correlate specifically with nickel-rich meals and may coexist with skin flares.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: Nickel can trigger mast cell degranulation, and some individuals with SNAS also meet criteria for MCAS. The two conditions can amplify each other, producing multi-system symptoms that are difficult to attribute to either condition alone.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Some high-nickel foods (such as fermented foods, certain fish, and processed meats) are also high in histamine, making it difficult to separate nickel reactivity from histamine intolerance. The two conditions can coexist and share overlapping symptom profiles.

related_conditions.name: Atopic dermatitis (eczema)
related_conditions.explanation: Atopic individuals have a higher prevalence of nickel sensitisation. Nickel-related skin flares, particularly dyshidrotic eczema, can be superimposed on a background of atopic eczema, complicating management.

related_conditions.name: IgE-mediated allergy
related_conditions.explanation: Nickel sensitivity is a type IV T-cell-mediated reaction, not IgE-mediated. It is commonly confused with true allergy in clinical settings, which can lead to inappropriate testing (e.g. skin prick tests or IgE panels that return negative) and dismissal of symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS)
related_conditions.explanation: Wholegrains — the primary targets of gluten-free diets — are also among the highest dietary sources of nickel. Some individuals attributed to NCGS may in fact be reacting to the nickel content of wheat and oats rather than gluten itself. Distinguishing these requires careful dietary challenge.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Emerging evidence suggests that gut microbiome composition may influence nickel absorption and systemic reactivity. Dysbiosis may lower the threshold for nickel-driven gut symptoms, and some SNAS patients show improvement in gut symptoms alongside microbiome-targeted interventions.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Like SNAS, coeliac disease improves on a diet that avoids wholegrains. Both conditions can present with GI symptoms and skin manifestations (dermatitis herpetiformis in coeliac; dyshidrotic eczema in SNAS). Coeliac serology and biopsy are needed to distinguish them.

related_conditions.name: Eosinophilic GI disorders
related_conditions.explanation: Both SNAS and eosinophilic disorders can produce GI inflammation in response to food antigens. Some overlap in presentation exists, and differentiating them requires endoscopic biopsy alongside dietary history.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Multi-system SNAS presentations with widespread pain and fatigue can overlap with fibromyalgia. Shared features include pain, fatigue, and a heightened systemic response to environmental triggers.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic systemic immune reactivity from SNAS may contribute to central nervous system sensitisation, lowering pain thresholds and amplifying symptom perception over time.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: Bloating, abdominal discomfort, and altered bowel habits from SNAS can prompt SIBO investigation. The two conditions can coexist, and addressing SIBO without identifying dietary nickel sensitivity may produce only partial improvement.

strategies.name: Low-nickel diet
strategies.description: Reducing intake of high-nickel foods — particularly oats, wholegrain products, legumes (lentils, chickpeas, kidney beans), nuts (especially peanuts and cashews), seeds, dark chocolate, soy products, spinach, and shellfish. The goal is to reduce daily nickel load below an individual threshold, not to achieve zero intake (which is impossible). Nickel content also varies by food preparation method and cookware.
strategies.notes: A strict low-nickel diet can inadvertently restrict fibre, magnesium, and plant protein. Working with a dietitian to maintain nutritional adequacy is important. Many people find a moderate reduction, rather than full elimination, sufficient to control symptoms.

strategies.name: Vitamin C with high-nickel meals
strategies.description: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) may reduce intestinal nickel absorption by forming a complex with nickel in the gut. Some individuals take 250–500 mg with high-nickel meals as a pragmatic strategy when dietary adherence is difficult.
strategies.notes: Studied in small trials; not a substitute for dietary management. High-dose vitamin C can cause GI side effects in sensitive individuals.

strategies.name: Iron sufficiency
strategies.description: Iron and nickel share transport mechanisms in the gut. Iron deficiency increases intestinal nickel absorption, meaning iron-deficient individuals may absorb significantly more dietary nickel. Correcting iron deficiency (under medical supervision) may reduce nickel absorption and systemic burden.
strategies.notes: Iron supplementation should be guided by serum ferritin and full blood count, not taken without testing. Excess iron carries its own risks.

strategies.name: Avoiding stainless steel and nickel-releasing cookware
strategies.description: Cooking acidic foods in stainless steel cookware can leach nickel into food, significantly increasing dietary nickel content. Using cast iron, ceramic-coated, or glass cookware reduces this source. Filtering tap water may also reduce nickel intake, particularly where water pipes are older.
strategies.notes: The contribution of cookware-leached nickel to total dietary intake varies widely and may be particularly significant for those who cook acidic dishes (tomato sauces, citrus-based dishes) frequently.

strategies.name: Contact source avoidance
strategies.description: Identifying and eliminating skin contact with nickel-releasing objects — switching to titanium, surgical steel, or gold jewellery; using barrier coatings on metal fasteners; covering nickel-containing watch backs and belt buckles; avoiding prolonged contact with phones with nickel-containing casings.
strategies.notes: Contact avoidance addresses the external sensitisation trigger but does not independently address dietary-driven systemic symptoms in SNAS.

strategies.name: Antihistamines for acute flares
strategies.description: Oral antihistamines (H1 blockers) can help manage acute itching and urticarial flares related to nickel reactivity. They do not address the underlying sensitisation or the GI component of SNAS.
strategies.notes: Useful for short-term symptom control, particularly of skin symptoms. Not a long-term solution and should be used under medical guidance.

strategies.name: Topical corticosteroids for skin flares
strategies.description: Short-course topical steroids can manage acute eczematous flares on the skin. Most useful as a bridge while dietary triggers are identified and reduced.
strategies.notes: Long-term use carries risks of skin thinning. The goal is to use dietary management to reduce the frequency and severity of flares requiring steroid treatment.

strategies.name: Oral hyposensitisation (nickel desensitisation)
strategies.description: A protocol developed primarily in Italian allergy centres in which gradually increasing oral doses of nickel sulfate are given over months to years with the aim of inducing tolerance. Still considered investigational and not widely available outside specialist research settings.
strategies.notes: Promising in early studies but not yet a standard clinical practice. Requires strict specialist supervision. Not appropriate for self-administration.

things_to_avoid: Cooking acidic foods in stainless steel pots and pans — nickel leaches into food, particularly in prolonged cooking
Nickel-releasing jewellery, particularly in freshly pierced ears where sensitisation is most likely to develop or worsen
Assuming a low-FODMAP diet will address nickel sensitivity — many high-FODMAP foods are not high in nickel, and many high-nickel foods are low in FODMAPs
Indiscriminate elimination of all plant foods — wholegrains and legumes are high in nickel but are also nutritionally important; unnecessarily broad restriction harms nutritional status
Relying solely on antihistamines or skin creams without addressing dietary nickel load — these manage symptoms rather than the underlying driver

Self-diagnosing based on contact allergy alone — a nickel jewellery reaction does not confirm SNAS, and dietary restriction without proper assessment is unnecessary for most people with contact-only allergy

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Many of the foods promoted as healthy — wholegrains, legumes, nuts, seeds, and dark chocolate — are among the highest dietary sources of nickel. Following a 'clean eating' or plant-heavy diet can significantly worsen SNAS symptoms.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People who have invested effort in eating what they believe to be a healthy diet are reluctant to hear that these foods may be driving their symptoms. There is also a social and identity dimension to diet that makes challenging it uncomfortable.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Many individuals find that a moderately reduced nickel intake — not total elimination of all healthy plant foods — is enough to bring symptoms below threshold. A dietitian can help identify which specific foods are highest-impact, allowing others to remain in the diet.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most mainstream gastroenterologists do not routinely test for or consider nickel sensitivity, and a negative standard GI workup does not rule it out. Many individuals with SNAS are told their gut symptoms are functional and sent away without further investigation.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People want a definitive diagnosis from a credible specialist. Being told 'everything is normal' by a gastroenterologist is often interpreted as authoritative closure, discouraging further investigation into alternative explanations.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who suspect SNAS after ruling out other gut conditions may need to seek out an allergist or dermatologist with a specific interest in contact and systemic metal allergy, or a functional medicine practitioner willing to trial a structured low-nickel diet. Referral pathways exist, but often require self-advocacy.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Patch testing positive for nickel does not mean every symptom is caused by nickel. Some individuals over-attribute symptoms to nickel once sensitisation is identified, leading to an overly restrictive diet with unnecessary nutritional risk.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Having a named explanation for years of unexplained symptoms is a relief, and there is a natural tendency to apply that explanation broadly. It feels counterproductive to question a diagnosis that finally seems to fit.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: A structured elimination and reintroduction protocol, ideally supervised by a dietitian, is the way to establish which symptoms are genuinely nickel-driven and which have other causes — including co-existing conditions like SIBO, histamine intolerance, or IBS.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: There is no cure for nickel sensitivity, and desensitisation protocols remain experimental and limited in availability. Management is long-term threshold reduction, not elimination of the problem.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People with chronic conditions naturally hope for a treatment that resolves the underlying problem. The idea of permanent dietary management without an endpoint is discouraging.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Many individuals achieve sustained symptom control and a good quality of life through dietary management alone. The threshold model is empowering once understood — it means occasional dietary 'exceptions' are manageable rather than catastrophic, and overall load matters more than perfect avoidance.

key_levers.lever: Structured low-nickel dietary trial with professional guidance
key_levers.why_it_matters: Dietary nickel reduction is the primary intervention for SNAS. Without a structured trial, it is impossible to know whether nickel is driving symptoms or how much reduction is needed.
key_levers.how_to_start: Seek a referral to a dietitian experienced in elimination diets or metal sensitivity, or to an allergist/dermatologist who manages SNAS. Obtain a reliable low-nickel food table (published Italian SNAS guidelines are widely used) and begin tracking intake and symptoms.
key_levers.timeline: Meaningful symptom improvement typically seen within 4–6 weeks of consistent reduction. Full stabilisation may take 2–3 months.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Without a confirmed positive patch test, dietary restriction may be unnecessary. Patch testing provides objective evidence of nickel sensitisation and is the foundation for a SNAS diagnosis.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a referral to a dermatologist or allergist who performs comprehensive patch testing. Ask specifically for the standard European or North American baseline patch test series, which includes nickel sulfate.
key_levers.timeline: Test results available within 4–5 days of application. Referral wait times vary by system and region.

key_levers.lever: Eliminating or reducing contact nickel sources
key_levers.why_it_matters: Ongoing skin contact with nickel-releasing objects provides continuous low-level immune stimulation that may lower the threshold for systemic reactivity. Reducing contact exposure supports dietary management.
key_levers.how_to_start: Use a DMG spot test kit to check jewellery, belt buckles, phone casings, and wristwatch backs. Replace confirmed nickel-releasing items with titanium, niobium, or high-carat gold alternatives.
key_levers.timeline: Contact dermatitis from eliminated sources typically improves within 2–4 weeks.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Iron deficiency significantly upregulates intestinal nickel absorption through shared transport proteins (DMT-1). Correcting deficiency may meaningfully reduce the amount of nickel absorbed from the same dietary intake.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request serum ferritin, iron, and full blood count from a GP. If ferritin is low or borderline, discuss appropriate iron supplementation.
key_levers.timeline: Ferritin levels take months to correct with oral supplementation; functional improvement in nickel absorption may follow.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Stainless steel cookware can leach significant amounts of nickel into food during cooking, especially acidic dishes. Switching cookware can reduce dietary nickel load without any change in food choices.
key_levers.how_to_start: Replace stainless steel pots and pans used for acidic dishes with cast iron (enamel-coated), ceramic, or glass alternatives. Avoid prolonged boiling or simmering in stainless steel.
key_levers.timeline: Immediate effect on nickel intake from cookware once switched.

common_mistakes: Conducting a dietary trial without a patch test — dietary restriction without confirmed sensitisation is unnecessary and may restrict nutrition without benefit
Eliminating all plant foods rather than focusing on the highest-nickel items — unnecessary restriction harms nutritional status and adherence
Expecting a single intervention (e.g. skin contact avoidance alone) to resolve systemic symptoms — SNAS requires addressing both contact and dietary nickel sources
Attributing all symptoms to nickel after a positive patch test without running a structured elimination trial — other conditions frequently coexist and may explain some or most symptoms
Abandoning the dietary trial too early — symptoms often improve gradually over 4–6 weeks; short trials of 1–2 weeks may not show the full response
Ignoring cookware and tap water as nickel sources while carefully managing food choices — these 'hidden' sources can keep nickel load above threshold despite dietary efforts
Self-restricting without dietitian guidance, leading to nutritional deficiencies in fibre, magnesium, folate, and plant protein

specialists.role: Performs patch testing to confirm nickel sensitisation, can diagnose SNAS and manage skin manifestations including dyshidrotic eczema and contact dermatitis
specialists.notes: The most common entry point for SNAS diagnosis. Not all dermatologists have deep expertise in systemic dietary implications of metal allergy — asking specifically about SNAS or dietary nickel management is worthwhile.

specialists.role: Can perform patch testing, oral nickel challenge, and manage multi-system presentations. More likely than general dermatologists to consider systemic dietary reactivity and co-existing conditions like MCAS or histamine intolerance.
specialists.notes: Specialist allergy centres with a focus on contact and occupational allergy are most likely to have SNAS-specific experience. Academic and university hospital allergy departments are often better resourced than general clinic settings.

specialists.type: Registered dietitian (specialising in elimination diets or allergy nutrition)
specialists.role: Designs and supervises structured low-nickel dietary trials, ensures nutritional adequacy during restriction, supports reintroduction, and helps identify hidden nickel sources in the diet
specialists.notes: Many dietitians are not specifically trained in nickel sensitivity. Seeking one with experience in food allergy, metal sensitivity, or therapeutic elimination diets is important. Functional dietitians or those working alongside allergy clinics are often the most familiar.

specialists.role: May investigate dietary drivers of chronic gut and skin symptoms, including nickel sensitivity, alongside other potential root causes. More likely than mainstream GPs to consider SNAS in the context of unexplained multi-system symptoms.
specialists.notes: Quality and approach vary significantly. Useful when mainstream medical workup has been unrevealing, but evidence-based practice standards should be verified.

specialists.role: Useful for ruling out structural GI causes and conditions that mimic SNAS (coeliac disease, IBD, eosinophilic disorders). Less likely to investigate nickel specifically unless aware of SNAS.
specialists.notes: Most gastroenterologists do not routinely test for dietary metal sensitivity. Their value is primarily in ruling out other diagnoses rather than in managing SNAS itself.

patient_communities.name: Nickel Allergy Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Several active Facebook groups dedicated to nickel allergy and SNAS exist. Members share low-nickel recipes, cookware recommendations, and experiences with diagnosis. Useful for practical day-to-day management tips, though medical advice shared in groups should always be verified.

patient_communities.name: r/NickelAllergy
patient_communities.notes: Community for people with nickel allergy and SNAS. Discussions cover diet, patch testing experiences, jewellery and cookware choices, and navigating diagnosis. Peer support and practical lived-experience information.

patient_communities.name: Nickel Allergy Network
patient_communities.notes: An international community focused on systemic nickel allergy, with particular interest in dietary management and advocacy for greater clinical recognition of SNAS.

learning_resources.title: The Nickel Allergy & SNAS Information Hub — American Contact Dermatitis Society
learning_resources.description: Professional society resources covering contact allergens including nickel. Useful for understanding the clinical basis of nickel sensitisation and patch testing. A starting point for understanding the medical framework around nickel allergy.

learning_resources.title: Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome: Nosologic Framework and Usefulness of Dietary Regimen
learning_resources.description: A foundational review paper defining the clinical scope of SNAS, its diagnostic criteria, and the evidence for dietary nickel restriction. Useful for those wanting to understand the scientific basis of the condition and bring evidence to clinical conversations.

learning_resources.title: Dietary Nickel Intake and Systemic Contact Dermatitis — a review
learning_resources.author_or_source: Jensen CS et al — Contact Dermatitis, 2003
learning_resources.description: Reviews the relationship between dietary nickel and systemic dermatitis, nickel content in foods, and evidence for low-nickel diets. A detailed reference for understanding nickel in the diet and its clinical implications.

learning_resources.title: Nickel Allergy: What You Need to Know — Mayo Clinic
learning_resources.description: Accessible overview of nickel allergy covering causes, symptoms, diagnosis, and management. A good starting point for people newly exploring nickel as a potential trigger, though it focuses more on contact allergy than systemic dietary reactivity.

learning_resources.title: Low Nickel Diet — Handout and Food Tables
learning_resources.author_or_source: Various allergy departments; Italian Nickel Allergy Study Group tables widely used
learning_resources.description: Published low-nickel food tables categorising foods by nickel content. Several versions exist from European dermatology and allergy centres; the Italian SNAS research group's tables are among the most comprehensive. Searching 'low nickel diet food table PDF' yields multiple clinical handouts. Essential practical reference for dietary management.

learning_resources.title: Nickel: The Hidden Metal in Your Diet — Podcast episode
learning_resources.author_or_source: Various functional nutrition and allergy podcasts
learning_resources.description: Several functional nutrition and integrative medicine podcasts have covered SNAS and dietary nickel sensitivity. Searching 'nickel sensitivity podcast' or 'SNAS diet' will surface current episodes. Audio format suits those who prefer listening while managing symptoms.

learning_resources.title: Contact Dermatitis — Textbook
learning_resources.author_or_source: Johansen JD, Mahler V, Lepoittevin JP, Frosch PJ (eds)
learning_resources.description: The definitive clinical textbook on contact dermatitis, with comprehensive chapters on nickel including epidemiology, mechanisms, diagnosis, and systemic reactions. Written for dermatologists and allergists but accessible to engaged lay readers seeking deep understanding.

sections.relevance_signal: Symptoms worse on a **vegan or wholefood diet** heavy in oats, legumes, nuts, seeds, chocolate. Two-phase reaction: gut symptoms at 1–3h then fatigue and joint aching 24–48h later.
